questions.text: What is the derivative of the logistic sigmoid function, σ'(z), expressed directly in terms of the function value σ(z)?
questions.explanation: Applying the quotient rule to the sigmoid function yields the expression e^-z / (1 + e^-z)^2. Rearranging terms matches the factorization σ(z) * (1 - σ(z)).

questions.text: Why does using Mean Squared Error (MSE) instead of Log-Loss to optimize a Sigmoid-activated classification model lead to slower convergence for highly incorrect predictions?
options: MSE loss functions cannot be calculated in spaces with dimensions greater than three.
The MSE gradient contains a sigmoid derivative term σ'(z) which saturates to zero when predictions are highly incorrect (p_i close to 1 when y_i is 0), eliminating updates.
MSE requires calculating the inverse Hessian at each step, stalling the optimizer.
Convexity forces MSE updates to proceed strictly in the positive weight quadrant.
questions.correct_answer: The MSE gradient contains a sigmoid derivative term σ'(z) which saturates to zero when predictions are highly incorrect (p_i close to 1 when y_i is 0), eliminating updates.
questions.explanation: The MSE gradient includes the term p_i(1 - p_i). If the prediction is confidently incorrect (e.g. p_i = 1 when the true target y_i = 0), this term is zero, making the gradient vanish. Under Log-Loss, this term cancels out, allowing the gradient to remain proportional to the prediction error (p_i - y_i).
